questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with a 6-day history of high fever (39.5°C), bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, red cracked lips, strawberry tongue, and a polymorphous rash on the trunk. On examination, there is a single enlarged cervical lymph node (>1.5 cm) and mild swelling of the hands and feet. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The patient meets four of the five clinical criteria for Kawasaki disease (fever >5 days, bilateral conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, cervical lymphadenopathy) plus extremity changes. Scarlet fever typically presents with a sandpaper rash and pharyngeal exudate, not conjunctivitis or extremity swelling. Measles has a prodromal cough, coryza, and Koplik spots. Juvenile idiopathic arthritis usually presents with arthritis and quotidian fevers but not conjunctivitis or strawberry tongue. A viral exanthem would not have this combination of features.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with a 6-day history of high fever, irritability, and a diffuse maculopapular rash. Examination reveals bilateral conjunctivitis without discharge. No oral changes or cervical lymphadenopathy are noted. The extremities are not swollen. Blood tests show raised inflammatory markers and mild pyuria. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Incomplete Kawasaki disease should be considered in infants with prolonged fever and fewer than four classic criteria, especially when elevated inflammatory markers and sterile pyuria are present. Fever >5 days plus bilateral conjunctivitis and rash meets the threshold for incomplete Kawasaki. UTI may cause fever and pyuria but not conjunctivitis. Roseola typically has fever then rash, without conjunctivitis. Meningococcal sepsis includes purpuric rash and rapid deterioration. Toxic shock syndrome has hypotension and multi-organ involvement.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl is referred to rheumatology after an echocardiogram performed for a heart murmur reveals a coronary artery aneurysm. Four weeks ago, she had a febrile illness lasting 8 days with red eyes and a diffuse rash, but no specific diagnosis was made at the time. She is now well. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of a coronary artery aneurysm in a young child with a history of prolonged fever, conjunctivitis, and rash is highly suggestive of Kawasaki disease, even if the acute presentation was atypical. Rheumatic fever typically causes valvular lesions, not coronary aneurysms. Myocarditis presents acutely with cardiac dysfunction. Coronary artery anomalies are congenital. Takayasu arteritis is rare in this age group and involves large vessels.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with a 7-day history of high fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, and a unilateral tender cervical lymph node. He also has symmetrical arthritis of both knees. There is no rash or desquamation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This patient has fever, conjunctivitis, oral changes, and lymphadenopathy—three of the four principal criteria (rash is absent but extremity changes may be subtle). Arthritis can occur in Kawasaki disease, and in older children the presentation can be incomplete. Systemic JIA presents with quotidian fever and evanescent rash, not conjunctivitis or strawberry tongue. Scarlet fever lacks conjunctivitis. Adenovirus may cause fever and conjunctivitis but not strawberry tongue or cervical lymphadenopathy. Leptospirosis has myalgia and headache.

questions.stem: A 1-year-old infant presents with a 7-day history of high fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, and a diffuse maculopapular rash. Two days ago, the fever resolved spontaneously, and now the fingers are peeling. On examination, the lips are dry and cracked, and there is a single enlarged cervical lymph node. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of prolonged fever, conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, cervical lymphadenopathy, and characteristic perineal or extremity desquamation is classic for Kawasaki disease. Measles has a prodrome of cough, coryza, and Koplik spots, and desquamation is not typical. Scarlet fever causes a sandpaper rash and desquamation but not conjunctivitis. Drug reaction and Stevens-Johnson syndrome involve mucosal erosions and target lesions, not this pattern.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with a 5-day history of fever, bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, erythematous cracked lips, and a polymorphous rash. He has no cervical lymphadenopathy or extremity changes. What is the minimum number of clinical criteria required to make a diagnosis of complete Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: The diagnosis of complete Kawasaki disease requires fever for at least 5 days plus at least 4 of the 5 principal clinical features. This patient has 3 features (conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash) so does not meet criteria; incomplete Kawasaki should be considered.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl is diagnosed with acute Kawasaki disease on day 6 of fever. She has received intravenous immunoglobulin and high-dose aspirin. Which investigation is most appropriate to assess for coronary artery involvement at this stage?
questions.explanation: Transthoracic echocardiography is the first-line imaging modality in acute Kawasaki disease to detect coronary artery dilatation or aneurysms. ECG and troponin are less specific, and CT/MRI are not first-line due to radiation or practicality.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child has had fever for 6 days, conjunctival injection, and strawberry tongue, but no other features of Kawasaki disease. Inflammatory markers are elevated. Which laboratory finding is most supportive of a diagnosis of incomplete Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Sterile pyuria is a supportive laboratory finding for incomplete Kawasaki disease. While elevated CRP and leukocytosis are common, they are non-specific; sterile pyuria is more specific and included in the AHA diagnostic algorithm.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old child with previous Kawasaki disease is followed up with echocardiography. The left anterior descending coronary artery measures 9 mm in internal diameter. Which of the following best describes this finding?
questions.explanation: In Kawasaki disease, a coronary artery aneurysm is considered giant if the internal diameter is ≥8 mm in children under 5 years, or ≥10 mm in older children. This case meets the criterion for giant aneurysm.

questions.stem: A 2-month-old infant presents with fever for 7 days, irritability, and a transient rash. No other mucocutaneous signs are present. Inflammatory markers are raised. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis of Kawasaki disease in this age group?
questions.explanation: Infants <6 months often present with incomplete Kawasaki disease. Echocardiography is key to detect coronary artery abnormalities (e.g., aneurysms) which, if present, confirm the diagnosis even without full clinical criteria. Other tests rule out infection but do not confirm Kawasaki.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with fever for 5 days, conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, cervical lymphadenopathy, and erythematous rash. Diagnosis: Kawasaki disease. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg single dose alone
Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg single dose plus high-dose aspirin 80 mg/kg/day
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend IVIG 2 g/kg single dose plus high-dose aspirin (80 mg/kg/day) as first-line treatment for acute Kawasaki disease. Aspirin alone is insufficient to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk, and steroids are reserved for refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old with Kawasaki disease received IVIG 2 g/kg and high-dose aspirin 48 hours ago. Fever persists and inflammatory markers remain elevated. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend a second dose of IVIG if fever persists or recurs after 36 hours of the first dose. Steroids or other immunomodulators are considered only after a failed second IVIG.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old with history of Kawasaki disease now has giant coronary artery aneurysms (diameter 9 mm) on echocardiography. She is asymptomatic. What long-term therapy is most appropriate to prevent thrombosis?
questions.explanation: For giant aneurysms (>8 mm), NICE recommends combined antiplatelet and anticoagulant therapy with low-dose aspirin and warfarin. Aspirin alone is insufficient to prevent thrombosis in this high-risk group.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old with acute Kawasaki disease has just been diagnosed with chickenpox (varicella). He has not yet started treatment. What is the most appropriate antiplatelet/anti-inflammatory management?
questions.explanation: Aspirin is contraindicated in children with active varicella due to Reye's syndrome risk. Clopidogrel is an alternative antiplatelet agent, while IVIG remains the anti-inflammatory cornerstone.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old with previous Kawasaki disease presents with acute chest pain and ST elevation on ECG. Coronary angiography shows thrombotic occlusion of a coronary artery aneurysm. What is the most appropriate reperfusion strategy?
questions.explanation: PCI is preferred over thrombolysis in children with Kawasaki-related acute MI due to lower bleeding risk and better outcomes. High-dose aspirin and heparin are adjunctive, not reperfusion.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with a 6-day history of high fever unresponsive to antipyretics. On examination, he has bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, erythematous cracked lips, a polymorphous rash on the trunk, and a single enlarged cervical lymph node (>1.5 cm). There is no swelling of the extremities. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Discuss with paediatric rheumatology within 24 hours
Take blood cultures and start broad-spectrum antibiotics
questions.explanation: This child meets classic criteria for Kawasaki disease (fever ≥5 days plus 4 of 5 features). NICE guidelines recommend immediate IVIG (2 g/kg single dose) to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk. Aspirin is adjunctive but not the first step; echo can follow, but IVIG should not be delayed.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl has had fever for 8 days. She has bilateral conjunctivitis and a red, swollen tongue, but no rash, lymphadenopathy, or extremity changes. Her inflammatory markers are elevated (CRP 120 mg/L, ESR 60 mm/hr). An echocardiogram yesterday showed normal coronary arteries. What is the most appropriate next action?
options: Reassess in 24 hours with no new treatment
questions.explanation: This is incomplete Kawasaki disease (fever plus only 2-3 features). NICE advises that patients with suspected incomplete Kawasaki and elevated inflammatory markers should still receive IVIG, because the risk of coronary aneurysms is significant even without full criteria.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child with Kawasaki disease diagnosed 7 days ago (treated with IVIG and aspirin) now develops hypotension (BP 70/40 mmHg), tachycardia, and cool peripheries. He has not passed urine for 6 hours. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give a 20 mL/kg intravenous fluid bolus
questions.explanation: The child is in shock. According to paediatric life support principles, the first step is fluid resuscitation (20 mL/kg bolus) to restore circulation. IVIG or antibiotics may be indicated later, but immediate action is to address hypotension. Echo can follow after stabilisation.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy with Kawasaki disease diagnosed 10 days ago has now completed IVIG and aspirin therapy. A follow-up echocardiogram reveals a 5 mm coronary artery aneurysm (z-score +4). He is well, afebrile, and has normal cardiac function. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start warfarin with a target INR 2-3
questions.explanation: For a small-to-medium coronary artery aneurysm, aspirin is first-line antiplatelet therapy per NICE. Warfarin is reserved for giant aneurysms (>8 mm) or thrombotic complications. Catheterisation is not routinely indicated. Repeat echo is planned, but starting aspirin is the immediate action.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a 7-day history of high fever, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, a diffuse erythematous rash, and cervical lymphadenopathy. She has no past medical history. Investigations show raised inflammatory markers (CRP 150 mg/L, ESR 70 mm/hr) and normal echocardiogram. She is clinically stable. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to rheumatology for outpatient follow-up
questions.explanation: Adult-onset Kawasaki disease is rare but managed similarly to paediatric disease. NICE recommends IVIG as first-line treatment regardless of age to prevent coronary aneurysms. Aspirin is adjunctive; prednisolone is reserved for IVIG-resistant cases. Viral serology might be considered but IVIG should not be delayed.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy presents with a 6-day history of fever, bilateral conjunctival injection, cracked lips, and a rash. On examination, he is tachycardic and hypotensive (blood pressure 70/40 mmHg). What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Kawasaki disease shock syndrome is a severe form of Kawasaki disease characterised by hypotension and poor perfusion, representing a red flag for immediate escalation. Toxic shock syndrome and septic shock are mimics but the conjunctival and oral findings are classic for Kawasaki.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl with a history of Kawasaki disease diagnosed at age 3, currently on low-dose aspirin, presents with acute central chest pain and nausea. ECG shows ST elevation in leads V1-V4. What is the most likely complication?
questions.explanation: Kawasaki disease can lead to coronary artery aneurysms, which may thrombose and cause myocardial infarction. ST elevation MI in a child with prior KD is highly suspicious for this. Myocarditis is more acute in the first weeks.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant has had fever for 6 days without any apparent source. There is no conjunctival injection, no rash, no lymphadenopathy, and the oral mucosa is normal. Which investigation finding would most strongly support a diagnosis of incomplete Kawasaki disease?
questions.explanation: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is diagnosed when fever persists and coronary artery abnormalities are found on echocardiogram, even without full clinical criteria. This is a red flag because infants are at high risk of aneurysm.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy presents with 5 days of fever, bilateral non-exudative conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, and cervical lymphadenopathy. He has no rash. Which of the following findings would be most indicative of Kawasaki disease in this age group?
options: Erythema and oedema of the hands and feet
questions.explanation: Erythema and oedema of the hands and feet is a classic acute feature of Kawasaki disease and, in combination with other criteria, confirms the diagnosis. Perianal desquamation is more common in infants, and fingertip desquamation occurs in the convalescent phase.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy with typical Kawasaki disease was treated with intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) and aspirin 24 hours ago. His temperature today is 39.0°C. Which clinical finding is a red flag for IVIG resistance and should prompt further management?
options: Fever >24 hours after completion of IVIG
questions.explanation: Persistent fever beyond 24 hours after IVIG is the key indicator of IVIG resistance, which carries a higher risk of coronary aneurysms and requires escalation (e.g., second dose of IVIG or steroids). The other findings are either expected or not critical thresholds.
